Airworthiness Limitations
entities.description: Mandatory replacement times and inspection periods for structural parts and components

entities.text: Life Time Limit: The airframe of the AQUILA AT01 is limited to 6000 hours of flight time.

An inspection program to reach an extension of replacement time can be obtained from AQUILA Aviation GmbH. For all S/N's having performed the 6000 h inspection program and possible maintenance actions resulting thereof, no further life time limit beyond 6000 operating hours will be established. The aircraft is then considered to have Safe Life.

entities.name: Airframe Structure
entities.description: Aircraft structural components and assemblies subject to mandatory replacement and inspection

entities.context: Life Time Limit for AQUILA AT01 airframe
Inspection program threshold for Safe Life extension

entities.text: All mandatory limitations listed have been determined by the airframe manufacturer. Compliance with the specified times and intervals is mandatory for maintaining the airworthiness of the aircraft.

THE AIRWORTHINESS LIMITATIONS CHAPTER IS APPROVED BY EUROPEAN AVIATION SAFETY AGENCY (EASA) IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE APPLICABLE CERTIFICATION PROCEDURES AND THE TYPE CERTIFICATION BASIS. THE AIRWORTHINESS LIMITATIONS CHAPTER IS FAA APPROVED AND SPECIFIES MAINTENANCE REQUIRED UNDER SECS. 43.16 AND 91.403 OF THE FEDERAL AVIATION REGULATIONS UNLESS AN ALTERNATIVE PROGRAM HAS BEEN FAA APPROVED.
For possible airworthiness limitations of engine, propeller, components and vendor equipment refer to the applicable maintenance data as listed in the Introduction chapter of this manual.

entities.text: The airframe of the AQUILA AT01 is limited to 6000 hours of flight time. For all S/N's having performed the 6000 h inspection program and possible maintenance actions resulting thereof, no further life time limit beyond 6000 operating hours will be established. The aircraft is then considered to have Safe Life.

All structural parts which are exposed to direct vertical sunlight and listed in the replacement requirements table have to be painted WHITE, excepting areas provided for registration marks, warnings and approved designs. This will prevent the temperature of the structure from becoming too high.
Where an interval is given in both flight time and calendar time, the limit which is reached first must be applied.

Ignition lock replacement required at 6000 flight hours
Starter relais replacement required at 2000 flight hours
Elevator control rods replacement required at 6000 flight hours, including rod ends
Rudder control cables replacement required at 6000 flight hours
Control surface plain bearing bushings replacement required at 6000 flight hours
Electrical fuel pump replacement required at 3000 flight hours or 10 calendar years, whichever is reached first
Nose landing gear spring package rubber elements replacement required at 5 calendar years
Main landing gear struts overhaul required at 6000 flight hours
Flexible hoses of the brake system replacement required at 10 calendar years
Lower rudder hinge bracket replacement required at 6000 flight hours
Wing attachment bolts replacement required at 6000 flight hours
Flexible teflon hoses of the oil/fuel system replacement required at 2000 flight hours or 15 calendar years, whichever is reached first. Hoses not covered by the engine type certificate.
Installation or removal of each item must be recorded in the aircraft logbook to monitor permissible service life.
Under certain circumstances the replacement or overhaul of components may be required before the time limits listed are reached.

entities.text: ROTAX mechanical fuel pump maintenance required every 5 years
ROTAX flexible teflon fuel hoses replacement required with engine overhaul
ROTAX rubber engine parts replacement required every 5 years
Spark plugs replacement required every 200 flight hours
Refer to the latest issues of BRP-Rotax, i.e. Service Bulletins, Service Information and to the ROTAX Aircraft Engines Maintenance Manual for ROTAX Engine Type 912 Series.
Refer to manufacturer instruction for overhauling.
Though there is no TBO for these instruments, the manufacturer Gebr. Winter GmbH & Co. KG recommends that airspeed indicators and altimeters are subjected to retesting after 5 years.
The 6 year calendar time limit applies for units not in continuous service with a minimum of 120 hours per year. Refer to the latest issue of the Woodward Service Bulletin S/B-33580-M.

For new aircraft and new engines the first check is carried out after 25 hours and should be of the extent of a 100-hour inspection. For new engines only an engine ground run and the checks listed in the "Engine" section have to be carried out.
If more than 30% of operation hours have been flown with leaded fuel (e.g. AVGAS 100LL), an additional 50-hour inspection is necessary (refer to ROTAX Aircraft Engines SI-912-016).
Annual inspections and 100 hour inspections on the AQUILA AT01 must include all inspection items as required by FAR 43, Appendix D, "Scope and detail of annual/100h inspections".
Maintenance intervals may be reduced if an aircraft is being operated under unusual environmental conditions.
The maintenance and checks listed are to be carried out at the specified intervals and documented appropriately.

Due to recent ROTAX publications the maintenance checks given for the ROTAX engine may not be up to date. Refer to the latest revisions of ROTAX Engine Type 912 Series Maintenance Manual and Service Bulletins.

Due to the multiplicity of equipment coming onto the market, no maintenance instructions are given for electronic equipment. For information on a certain component use the documentation provided by the manufacturer of this component.

entities.title: Canopy locking - Check canopy locking mechanism
entities.description: Check that canopy locking mechanism operates correctly. Check wear of parts. Check existence of the locking pin. The pin has to protrude the cover by approx. 2 mm. Cases of lacking locking pins have to be reported to the type certificate holder. Check function of the locking pin. The canopy locking mechanism must not be too smooth-running. In the locked position of the latch, a smooth running release of the latch due to in-flight vibrations must not be possible. If necessary, readjust locking pin.

Towing and Taxiing
TAXIING OF THE AIRCRAFT MUST BE CARRIED OUT BY AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY.
ALWAYS SELECT THE TAXI SPEED IN SUCH MANNER THAT IN THE EVENT OF BRAKE FAILURE A COLLISION WITH PERSONS OR STATIONARY OBJECTS IS EXCLUDED.
DO NOT OPERATE THE ENGINE AT HIGH RPM WHILE RUNNING UP OR TAXIING OVER GRAVEL, LOOSE STONES, OR ANY LOOSE MATERIAL, TO PREVENT ABRASION AND STONE DAMAGE TO THE PROPELLER BLADES.

entities.text: For storage periods between 7 and 30 days, engine oil level must be checked and topped to full mark, engine must be run for at least 5 minutes at 1200-1500 RPM, fuel tanks must be topped up, protective covers must be installed, and propeller must be rotated every 7 days through 6 revolutions.

For storage periods not exceeding 90 days, fuel tanks must be completely filled, engine preservation procedures per ROTAX manual must be followed, battery must be removed and stored in cool dry room, aircraft must be cleaned and waxed, brakes must be applied at least weekly, pitot tube cover must be installed, aircraft must be ventilated regularly, and landing gear must be jacked up with wheels rotated weekly.
Brakes must be applied frequently, at least once per week during extended storage.

Checking tire pressure regularly is the most important preventive measure in tire service. Improper tire pressure causes deterioration in the ground handling behavior of the aircraft and reduces the service life of the tire. Under-pressure is indicated by excessive wear in the tire shoulder area, over-pressure by excessive wear in the center of tire.

entities.text: Do not use silicone-based wax to polish the aircraft exterior.
Do not clean the aircraft at ambient temperatures close to 0°C with water.
Cover all lubricated parts during any cleaning process.
During the application of cleaners (e.g. cleaning the engine), the other surfaces must be covered carefully or otherwise protected.
NEVER USE GASOLINE, BENZENE, ALCOHOL, ACETONE, CARBON TETRACHLORIDE, LACQUER THINNER OR GLASS CLEANER. THESE MATERIALS WILL SOFTEN THE PLASTIC AND MAY CAUSE IT TO CRAZE.

If this type of lubricant is used the lubrication interval is reduced to annual.

KEEP WATER AWAY FROM PITOT AND STATIC PORTS AND ELECTRICAL AND AVIONIC EQUIPMENT.
DO NOT USE HARSH ABRASIVES, ALKALINE SOAPS OR DETERGENTS.
DO NOT USE CLEANING OR POLISHING AGENTS WHICH CONTAIN SILICONE.
After using cleaners containing any solvent or chemical, the relevant surfaces should always be waxed.
UNDER NO CIRCUMSTANCES USE ANY PETROLEUM BASED PRODUCT TO CLEAN THE LIGHTS.
Handle chemical cleaners and solvents with caution. Always read the manufacturer's instruction.
DO NOT SMOKE OR EXPOSE A FLAME WITHIN 100 FEET OF THE CLEANING AREA.
DO NOT USE GASOLINE OR OTHER HIGHLY FLAMMABLE SUBSTANCES.
DO NOT ATTEMPT TO WASH AN ENGINE WHEN IT IS STILL HOT OR RUNNING.
DO NOT DIRECT CLEANING SOLVENTS OR WATER STREAMS AT OPENINGS OF THE ALTERNATOR OR THE STARTER.
Clean surfaces before applying polish or wax.
If the aircraft is operated in a coastal or other salt-water environment, it must be washed and waxed more frequently.
A heavier wax layer on the leading edges of the wings and tail and on the cowl nose and propeller spinner will reduce abrasion in these areas.

entities.text: Eight cargo tie-down rings are provided to secure baggage. Baggage nets may be used to hold baggage in position during flight.
The aircraft is equipped with a Kannad 406 AF Compact ELT as standard. A different ELT may be installed; refer to equipment manufacturer manuals.
The ELT antenna is located on top of fuselage behind the cabin.

The ELT remote control panel/indicator (RCPI) is mounted on the instrument panel, right side. Both the front panel of the ELT and the RCPI are easily accessible from the pilot's normal seated position.
If equipped with a portable antenna, the ELT unit can be removed from the aircraft and used as a personal locating device when necessary to leave the aircraft after an accident.

Fuel system consists of two main integral wing fuel tanks, fuel selector/shut-off valve, auxiliary fuel pump with integrated filter, engine driven fuel pump, two single-barrel float type carburetors, flexible hoses and aluminum fuel lines

Fuel is delivered to carburetors by engine driven fuel pump from pre-selected tank. Electrical fuel pump provided as backup in case of engine pump failure. Excessive fuel flows through return lines back to same tank.
Fuel selector/shut-off valve provides tank selection and emergency fuel supply interruption. Handle mounted on center console between seats with LEFT, RIGHT, and OFF positions with positive detents.
Fuel quantity measured by capacitive or resistive type sensors and indicated by dual fuel level indicator. Fuel pressure measured at engine with low pressure warning light.
PERFORM ALL FUEL SYSTEM MAINTENANCE IN ACCORDANCE WITH SAFETY PRECAUTIONS CONTAINED IN 12-11-00!

entities.text: The electrical fuel pump has a filter screen which must be cleaned regularly. The filter screen can be removed for maintenance.

A mesh strainer is installed on the fuel outlet in each fuel tank. The strainer is accessible by opening the fuel tank rib access panel 610 BB / 510 BB. The strainer is brazed to a fitting that is installed in the fuel tank port. The fuel strainers in the fuel tanks should always be cleaned after aircraft has been in storage. If any damage or restrictions are noted, the strainer should be replaced.

The fuel system has a drain valve at its lowest point, namely at the base of the electrical fuel pump. The drain valve is accessible from outside the nose section without removing any component. It should be used regularly to check fuel for water and contamination.

Each wing fuel tank has a drain-valve at its base. The drain valves are accessible from outside at the bottom of the wings in the wing root area. They should be used regularly to check fuel for water and contamination.
A clean fuel distribution system is very important. The electrical fuel pump is incorporated into the system without a bypass.
Fuel strainers in fuel tanks should always be cleaned after aircraft has been in storage.
Drain valve at base of electrical fuel pump should be used regularly to check fuel for water and contamination.
Wing tank drain valves should be used regularly to check fuel for water and contamination.

entities.text: Accuracy of the indicating system strongly depends on the fuel grade used for calibration. Refer to SB-AT01-020 for further information. System calibration should be performed with unleaded super (RON 95) or super plus (RON 98) fuel grade. If AVGAS 100LL or AVGAS UL91 are used for calibration, an additional placard has to be installed on the instrument panel. Do not use mixtures of fuel grade for calibration.

For U.S. registered aircraft and aircraft registered where the FAA TC has been accepted, system calibration must be performed with AVGAS 100LL or AVGAS UL91 fuel grade. Do not use unleaded super (RON 95) or super plus (RON 98) or mixtures of fuel grade for calibration.
Fuel gauge calibrated with AVGAS. Indicated fuel level may differ from actual fuel level if MOGAS is used.

Senders without potentiometer are calibrated by using a magnet (type C) as shown in figure 202 or by applying a jumper between sender wire (yellow) and ground (type B). For these senders a second person is necessary for calibration as timing is important. One for using the magnet/jumper, the other one for switching the power.

entities.text: NEVER BLOW COMPRESSED AIR THROUGH PITOT OR STATIC LINES TOWARD INSTRUMENTS AS THIS CAN CAUSE DAMAGE TO INSTRUMENTS.
ENSURE THE PITOT HEAT (OPTIONAL) IS SWITCHED OFF AND THE TUBE HAS COOLED DOWN BEFORE TOUCHING THE TUBE. A HEATED PITOT TUBE CAN GET EXTREMELY HOT DURING OPERATION.
NEVER OPERATE PITOT HEATING WITHOUT THE CONTROL BOX AS THIS CAN DESTROY THE PITOT TUBE.
PLUG OR CAP INSTRUMENT PORTS IMMEDIATELY AFTER DISCONNECTING PITOT OR STATIC LINES TO PREVENT DIRT OR FOREIGN MATERIAL FROM ENTERING.

entities.text: Damage to composite structures is classified into four classes: Class 1 Severe extensive damage requiring partial component restoration or general component replacement; Class 2 Damage to primary or secondary structures with sandwich penetration; Class 3 Damage only to outer laminate layers on one side of sandwich without or only minor core gouging; Class 4 Damage to outer surface such as superficial scars or scratches with no holes or laminate fractures.

Only damages listed as minor can be repaired by qualified personnel without manufacturer-approved repair scheme. Minor damages are limited to: varnish or filler damage; damages not exceeding permissible hole, dent, and crack dimensions; damages not affecting glass and carbon rovings in primary structure; no Class 1 or critical area damages.

entities.text: Sanding fiber laminates results in a fine dust that may cause skin and/or respiratory irritation unless suitable protection is used.
Never handle fabric materials with bare hands, use clean cotton or rubber gloves.
Read and adhere to all manufacturer instructions, warnings and cautions on the materials and chemicals used.
Only damages classified as minor damages can be repaired by qualified personnel
Repairs must be completed by competent technicians trained in composite repair
All damages in force introduction or reinforced areas have to be carried out by certified repair stations or properly certified and trained persons
Repairs must be made in a clean, temperature controlled environment. Optimal repair temperature lies between 16°C and 27°C with 50% relative humidity or less
It is recommended to prepare a test specimen during actual repair which can be subjected to a destructive test to establish the quality of the adhesive bond in the repaired part

In general all damages in force introduction or reinforced areas (i.e. spars, ribs) have to be carried out by certified repair stations for composite aircraft structure works or properly certified and trained persons.
Class 4 damages don't have to be repaired immediately after detection if adequately protected from environmental conditions (e.g. adhesive tape).

Solvents used in repair processes are composed of a group of chemicals that often prove toxic. To avoid toxic poisoning, work in a well-ventilated area only and always be alert for symptoms of poisoning. If symptoms are observed, it is vital to immediately remove the person from the contaminated area.
Protective clothing should be worn to avoid skin contact with chemical substances.
Many of the chemicals used are flammable.
All control surfaces have to be checked for weight and control surface moment after repair.
Class 4 damages do not need to be repaired immediately after detection if adequately protected from environmental conditions.

entities.text: Blade shake (fore and aft movement) greater than 3 mm requires return to factory or approved repair station to correct the pre-load of the blade retention bearing.
Blade angle play greater than 2° indicates blade loose due to bearing setting and/or increased wear in pitch change mechanism. Return propeller to factory or approved repair station.
Trapped air in propeller piston causes surging rpm. Move propeller control at least twice before flying at about 1800 rpm with a drop of about 500 rpm.
Sludge deposit in propeller piston and governor requires cleaning of oil tubes. Contact manufacturer.
The propeller governor keeps the pre-selected propeller speed at a constant value, regardless of manifold pressure and airspeed.
Turning the check nut loose by ¼ turn will increase rpm by approximately 100 rpm.

entities.text: If sudden oil leakage occurs, move power lever back until the rpm decreases. In this condition the propeller goes back to the low pitch stop automatically and no oil pressure is needed.

Propeller rpm should be always lower than adjusted with the propeller control. This will hold the governor in an underspeed condition and no oil pressure will be transferred from the governor to the propeller.

If the cause cannot be found in the fuel system the flight may be continued when throttle setting is reduced, avoiding excessive manifold pressure and overheating of the engine. The rpm will remain low because the propeller pitch is on the high pitch stop.

entities.text: The ignition unit is completely maintenance-free and needs no external power supply.
With the switch OFF, the charging coils are grounded and will not supply capacitors with current.
During normal engine operation, the switch is in the BOTH position.
The switch is moved to L or R only to check the ignition circuits or if one ignition circuit is not working properly.

To start the engine, the switch should be rotated to the right into the spring-loaded START position, activating the starter if ALT/BAT is ON. When released, it automatically returns to the BOTH position.
